---
title: "Creating a Website with Quarto"
description: "A summary of how to create a website with Quarto, from installing Quarto to publishing with GitHub Pages."
date: "2026-02-07"
date-modified: "2026-02-07"
draft: true
lang: en
categories: [quarto]
---

::: {.callout-note}
Original Japanese version: [Quartoでウェブサイトを作成する方法](../../../posts/2026-02-07-quarto-website/index.qmd)
:::

[Quarto](https://quarto.org/) is an open-source tool for creating documents and websites using languages such as R and Python.
This article summarizes how to create a website with Quarto, from installing Quarto to publishing with GitHub Pages.

It mostly follows the [official documentation](https://quarto.org/), with some added notes and cautions.

## About Quarto

### Overview

In short, Quarto is a tool that can output documents containing code written in R or Python to formats such as HTML, PDF, and Word.
It is positioned especially as a successor to [R Markdown](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/), so it is familiar to R users.
Quarto also integrates with integrated development environments, or IDEs, such as RStudio and VS Code, making it easy to use.

### Differences from R Markdown

R Markdown and Quarto are similar, but Quarto provides more flexible and powerful features.

Based on [FAQ for R Markdown Users - Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/faq/rmarkdown.html), I summarize the differences in my own way.

#### A Unified Document Creation System

Originally, there was R Markdown, which could handle Markdown files containing R code.
There were also packages such as [`blogdown`](https://pkgs.rstudio.com/blogdown/) for creating websites, [`distill`](https://rstudio.github.io/distill/figures.html), and [`bookdown`](https://pkgs.rstudio.com/bookdown/) for creating books.
One purpose of Quarto is to unify this situation, where systems for creating various documents with R Markdown were scattered across packages.

#### Support for Programming Languages Other Than R

Support for languages other than R is another major feature.
As its name suggests, R Markdown was specialized for the R language, but Quarto supports other languages such as Python and Julia.
This allows users of different languages to use Quarto, and it also lets people who work with multiple languages create documents involving those languages with a single tool.

::: {.callout-note}
Because I use both R and Python, this feature of Quarto was very attractive.
It was one of the main reasons I decided to move to Quarto.
:::

#### The Future of R Markdown

Quarto integrates many R Markdown features, but R Markdown itself is not being discontinued.
R Markdown is already used by many users, and existing documents will not disappear, nor will it become impossible to create new ones.

However, it seems that the expansion of new features will focus on Quarto.
For these points, see Yihui Xie's article, [With Quarto Coming, is R Markdown Going Away? No.](https://yihui.org/en/2022/04/quarto-r-markdown/).

::: {.callout-note}
Yihui Xie worked at RStudio/Posit until 2023 and is one of the main developers of R Markdown.
:::

#### Learning Cost of Quarto

Quarto syntax is very similar to R Markdown, and in most cases R Markdown knowledge can be used directly.
There are Quarto-specific features, but the basic usage is the same as R Markdown.
For that reason, the learning cost is low for people who are already familiar with R Markdown.

Also, people currently using R Markdown do not need to force themselves to migrate to Quarto.
This is also emphasized in the official documentation.
However, when starting a new project, Quarto is worth considering.

::: {.callout-note}
When Quarto first appeared, I was also using R Markdown.
For a while, I kept using R Markdown while gradually trying Quarto.
Recently, I use Quarto for new projects.
:::

### Summary of Quarto

- Successor to R Markdown
- Unifies document creation systems that were scattered across R Markdown-related packages
- Supports multiple programming languages such as R and Python
- No need to force a migration from R Markdown, but the learning cost is not very high

## Installing Quarto

Install it by following [Get Started - Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/).

Quarto is provided as a standalone application and is independent of R and Python.
Download and install the Quarto CLI for your operating system from the link above.

::: {.callout-note}
## What Is a CLI?

CLI stands for Command Line Interface.
In simple terms, it is a way of typing and running commands in a black screen such as a terminal or command prompt.

The opposite term is GUI, or Graphical User Interface, which is a way of operating visually with a mouse and keyboard.
Quarto is provided as a CLI, but you do not need to memorize complicated commands, and most operations can also be done through a GUI.
:::

## Using Quarto in an Editor

Quarto can be used together with integrated development environments such as RStudio and VS Code.
The official documentation provides instructions for the following tools.

- Positron
- Visual Studio Code (VS Code)
- Jupyter
- RStudio
- Neovim
- Text Editor

I mainly use Positron.
Here, I summarize Positron and RStudio.

### Using Quarto in Positron

#### What Is Positron?

Positron is a free IDE provided by Posit, with enhanced integration with Quarto.
It is based on [Code - OSS](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode), the open-source version of [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/), with added features specialized for R and Python.

It is positioned somewhat like a successor to RStudio, but it does not completely replace it.

For details such as differences from RStudio, see the official documentation below.

- [Frequently Asked Questions - Positron](https://positron.posit.co/faqs.html#how-is-positron-different-from-rstudio-vs-code-or-jupyter-notebooks)

#### Creating a qmd File

Create a new file and save it with an appropriate name.
At this time, use the `.qmd` extension.
For example, `test.qmd`.

#### Creating a Quarto Document

Open the created qmd file and write the following.

````{.r filename="test.qmd"}
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Heading 1

## Heading 2

### Heading 3

This is my first Quarto document.
Japanese can also be written here.

## Code

Write a code block as follows.

```{r}
#| label: example
plot(iris[, 1:2], col = iris$Species)
```

## Bullet List

- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3
````

After writing some content, click the Preview button in the upper left.

::: {.callout-note}
The shortcut for Preview is `Ctrl+Shift+K` on Windows/Linux or `Cmd+Shift+K` on Mac.
:::

## Reference

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org/)
